speaker: interviewer
text: --1995. You had gotten off the train at Majdanek.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And the-- the-- the-- generally, the-- the people were chased. We had to run. And we had these knapsacks, which was an indication of our undying optimism, because we didn't know what-- what-- what would happen to us ultimately.

speaker: interviewer
text: And finally, we got to this-- this no man's land, which I described before-- the four camps that were adjacent to each other, four camps for men, and one for women. And between those there was a kind of limbo. OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: There we stayed another few days, I think about three. Three was the magic number. It always is the magic number for good things, too. And-- and we were starving. And we didn't have any food in the train either. So we-- my father and I drank water from the puddles, you know, the rain puddles. And then we were separated. Men and women were separated. My father was taken to the gas chamber. We didn't know it. But we were told from a distance, a week or so later, men and women were separated. But when we worked, we walked-- actually, there was no work in Majdanek. I'll explain it in a moment. We-- we marched in groups. And so once, a man screamed to us, your father was killed, so we knew. We were in close proximity to each other, so it was not a great distance.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, we-- we kept on asking, do you know what happened to this one? Do you know what happened to that one? So they told us that my father was killed in the gas chamber.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I often, till this day-- and I guess as I'm growing old, it-- it becomes-- the vision is more immediate. I see my father in the gas chamber, and it's a-- it's a very painful image. You know, people were scratching the walls. It was a slow death, 20 minutes, a half an hour. And before he was taken to the gas chamber-- when we were still in this no man's land, having been the eldest, you know, the oldest sister, he charged me with taking care of my mother and sister. He, who always took care of me, asked me to take care of my mother and sister. Actually, they took care of me because I became quite sick later. And so then we were-- then we went to the showers, my mother, sister, and I. And we didn't know which showers were-- we already knew about the gas chambers because my father told us. And other people knew through other sources, obviously not through my father. And we didn't know-- we knew that there were gas chambers. We knew that there were showers. But fortunately, we passed the selection. The selection was conducted by men only. And they were leering at us, you know, and making jokes. And fortunately, my sister and I had little breasts already, you know, so we didn't look like children. And they were-- you know, and-- and so they let us pass. The selections were in the nude. And indeed, we did go into the shower rather than into the shower of gas. We went into the shower of water. And in fact, I found one of my shoes when I came out. And we didn't have striped clothes in this place. Whatever we could find-- there was a pile of clothes-- whatever we could find, we just grabbed and got dressed. And then they took us to a-- to the barracks, you know, to the campgrounds and the barracks. And there were about 500 women in each barrack. And there were three levels-- the-- the bunks were three levels high. And every morning, at the crack of dawn, we were chased out of the barracks, and there were selections. And so there were, say, 500 women-- or 300 women in the barrack, if some of the women had already been killed, and there was no transport of new people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we would-- they made us line up in rows of five, one behind the other-- long rows, horizontally. And every day, they would select people to go to the gas chamber. Apparently, they could not accommodate everyone because the gas chamber wasn't large enough. So this was the nature of the death, you know, progression of death. You know, one group went and another group.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was actually no work in-- in Majdanek. It was a death camp. And we didn't have any latrines. So we had barrels behind each barrack into which we relieved ourselves. And sometimes the guards on all four corners of the camp-- all the camps were square-- maybe not square, but they had four corners. They would shoot at us for sport. You know, the guards in the-- in the towers would shoot at us for sport. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and then the only other work was digging potatoes in the fields. This was in May. June-- May-- June wasn't so bad, but May was extremely cold, and we had very scanty clothing, very little to wear. We had no coats or anything. Whatever we grabbed, we were able to-- to-- to take. And we had no names. We had numbers. And in some way, ironically, even though this was a form of dehumanization, it helped us because nobody knew that my mother, my sister, and I were related to each other because they would've definitely separated us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we used to dig the potatoes and sit very close to each other. Then we also carried grass, you know, strips of grass, from one place the other.

speaker: interviewee
text: In addition to that, there was a huge crater in Majdanek. This was outside of the camp. Now, my sister-- my mother, my sister, and I tried never to be separated. But sometimes we had no choice.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one day, my mother was made to stay on the campgrounds, and my sister and I were made to go to this huge crater. It was really enormous. And in that crater-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm trying to give you more or less the dimensions-- maybe like a small baseball field. I'm not too familiar with baseball, so I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, it was huge. It was not terribly deep. But in it were all kinds of human possessions, Jewish human possessions-- hair, teeth, you know, bridges, eyeglasses, books-- lots of books-- and various kinds of cloth articles. You know. But-- but they were piled up in separate piles. And I was working there with my sister. We were putting-- we were making some order because for the Germans, [GERMAN], which means, 'there must be order.'

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were putting these eyeglasses together in one pile, the underwear together, the outerwear, the shoes, the books, whatever else. And there was a woman next to me-- my sister was working at another pile. As I said, we tried not to be separated, but sometimes they assigned us to these things.

speaker: interviewee
text: A woman next to me found a gold coin. I think it was a Russian ruble. Apparently somebody tried to hide it, hoping that if they have the money or the gold, they'll be able to buy their lives. And this-- there was a German SS woman. Her name was Brigida. As a matter of fact, she survived the war. I was supposed to testify against her, but I didn't remember her. And I didn't want to lie because I might have ruined the whole thing. And besides, I didn't want to lie anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she-- she used to-- she was flanked by dogs, police dogs. And-- and she used to sic them on people. Sic them-- that's the word, is it not? And she-- when the woman next to me noticed the Brigida on top of that crater, she threw the coin away.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Brigitta had, literally, X-ray eyes. She was-- there was something diabolic about her. And she took us both because she didn't know which one of us had thrown the gold coin. She took us to the top of the crater, and she gave us a savage beating. I remember my beating. She beat me with an iron rod. And there was a kapo. Kapos were the-- you know, the people who--

speaker: interviewer
text: The supervisors?

speaker: interviewee
text: --Supervisor, something like that. It comes from the Italian word hat, OK? Head, rather. And so she-- she gave Brigida a wooden stick, with which she beat me. And I tried to be clever, and I stuck my feet out because I had one shoe that was my own and one that was not. But she was just as clever as I, and she beat me savagely. I was bleeding, and I-- my left arm was full of welts. And then-- and my sister stood there, and she watched this, and she couldn't do anything. So you can imagine her anguish. Sometimes when I tell this to my students who are college students, they don't understand. And they are appalled that my sister didn't come to my rescue. They don't understand that both of us would have been shot. And-- and-- and had my sister done anything, I would've been shot on the spot. And the best thing to do was to stay as-- you know, to-- to-- not to move. And I have a hard time convincing them, you know, that that was the only-- that there was no other recourse. And then finally, you know, she let me go. And I could barely walk. My-- whatever clothes I had was in shreds. And then we came to the camp, and there was a selection. There were selections every morning. And very often, when we came back from-- from whatever work we did, there were selections, too. As I recall, they were not regular-- that is to say, they were not each time. But this time, there was a selection. And you know, my sister and another woman had to support me because I couldn't walk. And I don't know how I passed the gates. I just don't know how. I don't believe in miracles. I'm a rationalist. But I somehow passed the gate. And then I wasn't able to work, and I had hide out all the time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Once there was a savage selection during which-- there were Polish women in the camp, too-- during which a Polish woman lost her eye, you know, she was beaten so savagely. Now, because my mother insisted that both my sister and I take a spoon of her soup whenever the soup was doled out, which was once a day, and a ration of bread in the morning. You know, it was a starvation diet. But my mother was a mother. So we used to quarrel with her because we didn't want to take her soup. And so we-- we created a little bit of noise, as a result of which we were sent to a punitive barrack in Majdanek that was run by a Polish woman, and there was a kapo who was very, very sadistic. She kind of took a liking to me because I was maybe a little bit more gentle than some people. I didn't push so much for the soup. Because you lined up for the soup. And you had to be in the right place because if you were the first one on line, you got very thin soup. If you were the last one, and the soup was thicker on the bottom of the barrel, you know, then you wouldn't get anything. You know. So you had to find a strategy.

speaker: interviewer
text: But one-- so we all tried. I mean, if you didn't try, you couldn't live. OK? If you gave up, you couldn't live. If you didn't believe that you're going to survive, there was just no way that you could survive, which is not to say that we did anything extraordinary because everything was largely by chance. People were killed largely by chance because they were in that particular place-- everybody was to be killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: But if you didn't have that gumption, if you didn't have that-- that will-- and I think my will came from the fact that I was with my mother and sister because we were like-- we were like one organism. We were literally breathing for one another. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] So we were sent to the punitive barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: All three of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: All three of us, right, because we were-- they were called us 'the monkeys,' because we quarreled about the soup. And one day-- there were 500 of us in the barracks, three levels high. And we slept in one bunk, the three of us, because there was no straw anymore, and the cold was just unspeakable. And one day, there was a bit of humming-- you know, people were talking, so there was noise in the barracks. And she constantly screamed, be quiet, be quiet-- you have 500 people in the barrack-- even if you-- if you sneeze, or if you say a word, move or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she took me out of the-- and she said, I'm going to give this child-- 24-- I'll whip her 24 times, 25 times. What is called? I'll give her-- I'll-- with the-- I'll lash her 25 times if there's no absolute silence. So obviously, the women were absolutely silent, and obviously I was terrified. But she, in some way, sadistic and terrible as she was, saved my life and my mother's and my sister's, and I will tell you how.

speaker: interviewee
text: But one day-- again, we were separated. My mother and sister were working outside. I was working inside. A German came from another camp. He was not an SS man. He was wearing civilian clothes. And he was running-- I don't know whether he was running a factory, but he was affiliated with an ammunition factory in another camp, and they needed slave labor.

speaker: interviewer
text: So those women who were on the campgrounds were picked as a group to go to this camp. And we asked him, and he says, don't worry. You are not going to be killed, but you're going to another camp. And I rejoiced. I was very happy about it. But then, my goodness, where are my mother and my sister? And when they came, I told them about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my sister, only about five or six years ago, told me that there were-- she was surprised that I didn't know-- that they were convinced that I was going to the gas chamber, that the whole thing was a ruse, the whole thing was a lie. And they did not want me to die by myself. So they changed places with two other women who also believed that they were going to the gas chamber. I did not. Maybe in my naivete, I was wise. Maybe, but I'm sure it was just an accident. I just-- the measure of optimism. So these two women were very happy to change places with my mother and sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: My mother and sister never told me what they were doing. I don't think I would have-- I would have stopped them because we were one. You know? Without even articulating this oneness, you know, without promising each other, we just knew it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother-- and indeed, we were sent to this other camp. So my mother, sister, and I survived. And these two women were probably killed, which is not a happy thought to live with, even though we had really-- you know, my mother and sister wanted to die with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were sent to this other camp, which was-- you know, the-- the factory was an ammunition factory. We produced weapons there. And when we first arrived-- the name of that camp is Skarzysko, If you want to spell it. S-K-A-R-Z-Y-S-K-O, OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, there were three fields, three camps. And fortunately, we-- we were sent to the best one. Werka, W-E-R-K-A. 'Werka' means section-- work, actually. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when we first arrived, they did not have-- they did not have barracks for us in this camp. We were also in the train for endless days. I don't remember how many.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they put us in something that might have been a barracks before, but it looked like a chicken coop to me when I walked in because the-- the-- the-- the cots-- not the cots. What do you call-- the three-level bunk beds were organized in a peculiar way. They had doors. In other words, they were enclosed. OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were rats galore. I mean, there were so many rats all over the place. They were jumping all over us. And we stayed there for some time because the barracks were being built for us. We were called the [POLISH] people, 'KL' people, because we came from a concentration camp. And Majdanek did not have the numbers, did not tattoo people. The only people who are tattooed were men, maybe women, too, who had been there a long time. And they had a tattoo here.

speaker: interviewer
text: But we were there to stay for a short time because we were going to go to the gas chamber. So they didn't bother tattooing us with a KL. OK? So ultimately, they built the bunkers for us, and we-- not the bunkers-- the barracks for us. And the barracks were built out of planks of wood with holes in them. You know, wood had a tendency to have these-- Knots?

speaker: interviewee
text: --Knots, right. And the only insulation was straw at the lower, at about three feet from the ground. And there lice and rats bred, but mostly-- mostly not so much lice as rats. And we had a stove and a little-- you know, with a pipe that went out. And we had to get-- I don't know how we got the wood. We worked 12-hour shifts, sometimes more in the factory. And I think perhaps the most memorable thing for me is the fact that on these bunk beds, we composed songs and poetry. We wrote-- we didn't write because we didn't have writing materials. But we-- we-- we composed songs, you know, in the mind, memories, and actually poetry-- poems. And we sang them to old banal tunes, to tangos. You know. And we laughed, and were hungry. And there was one woman-- she was a young woman. She was a rabbi's daughter. She was so hungry. She was pregnant. She came pregnant to-- to Majdanek and then to Skarzysko. But it was not visible yet because it was the beginning of her pregnancy. But then, of course, her stomach began to grow. And she ate lice because she was so hungry. Some of us thought she was mad. I didn't. I still don't. She survived. But she left her baby. She gave birth to her baby in a lavatory which was made out of a plank of wood and little holes. And it was like a cesspool, and that's where she gave birth. And there-- you see--

speaker: interviewer
text: And she left the baby there?

speaker: interviewee
text: She left the baby there. What could she do? You know. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The fact that we-- that we composed these-- that we wrote-- I mean, we didn't write-- that we produced poems was another vehicle of-- of-- of resistance. You know, we even joked. We even laughed under these terrible circumstances. Even at this point-- shortly be-- shortly after this point-- but actually, it was part of this point because it was a gradual thing that I'm going to describe in a minute. Because of the lice, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they crawled on the walls, literally. We were covered-- actually, in Majdanek, we were already covered with lice. I developed scabies. It's a disease that forms terrible sores between the fingers. And then I was covered with all sorts of sores. Everybody was covered-- virtually everybody was covered with-- with sores, especially the people from Majdanek, the women from Majdanek. Men were separated. They were in another place. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I also developed some sort of a mange. There's a word for it. It's called [POLISH] in Polish. Which-- which settles in the hair, in the head. And it's-- and as a result of it-- in other words, the lice breed there in the pus. OK? So on my left cheek, I had practically no skin. I had terrible, terrible sores. And on my neck, there were like-- like-- like a coat of armor. And the itching was so indescribable. It was so excruciating. So I couldn't scratch because the-- the-- the-- the-- the thing would fall off. The scab fell off. So I used to turn my head, which also would cause the sores-- you know, the scabs to fall off.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ultimately, I had typhus, I think, or typhoid fever. I'm very confused about the two, always. I somehow never can get it straight. I wonder why. Because I had one of these when I was a child. But it was not as a result of filth but rather, there was an epidemic-- I was four or five years old-- before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was put in a hospital. Now let me describe the hospital. It was a barrack. It was in Skarzysko. I was like a leper. People were afraid to look at me.

speaker: interviewee
text: My hair was shaved. I don't remember when it was shaved, nor do I want to remember. Sometimes I'm tempted to ask my sister when it was shaved. This was not the worst thing that happened to me, obviously. But for some reason, I want to keep this secret from myself. Sometimes it's better not to remember everything, contrary to modern psychology, catharsis, remember, remember. But for us, it is not the catharsis. For us, it is we lived pain. We lived anguish. So I don't ask her. I don't want to trouble her with the memory. And I was put in that hospital. And because I was-- I really smelled to high heaven. You know, pus has a very, very powerful odor. And because I looked like a leper, as I said, I was put at the end of the barrack. It was just the barrack like all the other barracks, but in it were men and women. And it was very fortunate that I was put towards the very end of the barrack where people, you know, relieved themselves. There were barrels there, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Ger-- there was a German who ran the-- who was the-- the-- the commandant of the-- actually, he was-- he was a German national. But when they came out to weed us out, you know, to take us, there was no-- there were no gas chambers. But there were mass-- there was a mass grave. And when they came to weed us out of there, [BACKGROUND NOISE] they never went far enough to find me. OK? So that saved my life. Ironically enough, that saved my life.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then ultimately I left. And then we'd learned that there were two cousins in that concentration camp. And then we were sent to another concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And incidentally, my life was saved because one day my sister poured out a can of urine. OK? Now, the lavatories were very, very far, and it was bitter cold. And we didn't have any clothes. What I was wearing at the time was a housecoat, a huge flannel housecoat that was full of blood of menses of a woman, the period of the woman, in the back. Of course we couldn't wash it. My hair was shaved, so I was-- I didn't get a scarf, but I got a bolero, a very heavy rayon bolero. And we used to tie it in the front, you may have seen, and make a knot here. And once somebody said, who's that devil with the knot on her head? I just didn't look human. And one-- oh, yeah. So we ate from these [POLISH], we used to call it, from these cans. OK? And my sister urinated into it because it was so bitter cold, and we had clogs, and the snow would get stuck, and the clogs were enormous. They were all one size. And you-- it was a kind of seesaw. So as I said for the third time now, she urinated there, and she poured out the urine. Now, in this filth, in this-- this terrible place, the Germans made the kapos, the Jewish police, keep the grounds clean. And-- and it was January or February, so when she poured out the urine, it was yellow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so-- and so the Jewish policeman came to us, and he beat my mother-- he beat my sister for it. And my mother said that her blood will not rest in the grave, as a result of which he saved me several times from selections.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a matter of fact, my mother was a very brave woman. When we first arrived, there was a Jewish policeman who really wasn't nice, to say the least. And he was beating some Jewish people. And my mother screamed out to him, how could you, you, another Jew, do that? You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ultimately, he was killed in Buchenwald by-- you know, as-- as revenge-- not revenge-- punishment, I suppose. There were not too many, but there were some who-- and I have no pity for them. And I-- and I make no apologies for them. You know. After all, we're entitled to-- to awful people, too. Although perhaps it's not the most moral thing to do. Everybody should be perfect. But I guess we are not. Then we were sent to another camp. Oh no, in this camp, there was no soap. There was no water. We were taken to the showers every so often in the factory. My sister was very brave. She was a little girl of 13, 14 years old. And she used to steal soapsuds from the machines which washed the bullets, you know, the-- the shells, the bullet shells. One day we sneaked into the lavatory. In-- in the-- in the-- in the factory, you know, there was a lavatory. And she wanted to wash her hair. And in my terror, I forgot to add water to the-- the suds were burning, that soap, that running soap. They were burning suds. They came out really hot. Hot is not the word, just burning. And I forgot to add water to it. And I poured it over her head. She already had sores, also scabies. And so this exacerbated the problem. And she developed terrible sores. And there was a Jewish couple who wanted to shave her hair. And my sister [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] stayed up nights-- not-- she didn't sleep. It was a vigil not to be caught. And ultimately, the kapo relented and let her go. But she actually wanted to shave my sister's hair for my sister's sake because she thought that the sores had a better chance of being-- not cured, but of not getting further aggravated if she didn't have the hair. All right, then we were sent to another camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I just get the timing from you?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, actually we should change tapes while we're between places.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. But how-- when did you go-- when did you go to Majdanek?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the very end of April, maybe the-- the very beginning of May in 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And then when did you go to Skarzysko?

speaker: interviewee
text: Skarzysko-- Skarzysko, I think they-- we didn't have calendars. Don't forget.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, I know.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was in August. I think we were in Majdanek a few months.

speaker: interviewer
text: A few months.

speaker: interviewee
text: A few months. So I know that they-- that they found us in the-- the resistance started April 19. And we were uncovered towards the end. They found us towards the end. Although the resistance went on for months after, but when the ghetto was burning. So it must've been the very end of April. And by the time we got to Majdanek, it may have been the beginning of-- of May.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in fact, people were coming-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with-- you know, other transports with people with terrible burns, people who jumped out the windows in the Warsaw Ghetto to escape the burning, you know, the-- the fires. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Are we still on?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's-- Yeah, let's stop.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's stop, I think, because we're-- so we don't--
